claims.flip_evidence_text: (v) as set forth in Section 5.1 of the Company Disclosure Letter
claims.notes: The claim correctly identifies that Buyer (Parent) consent is required for actions outside the Ordinary Course of Business and accurately lists exceptions (i), (iii), and (iv). However, it omits exception (v), which permits actions set forth in Section 5.1 of the Company Disclosure Letter without consent.

prompt:
You are a proxy legal annotator for a research pilot. Use only the contract excerpt, the question, the model answer, and the fixed claim list.

For each claim return:
- correctness_label: true, false, or partially_true
- load_bearing_label: yes or no
- flip_evidence_text: required if load_bearing_label=yes, else empty string
- notes: optional short note

Contract excerpt:
Section 5.1 Conduct of the Company. (a) From and after the date hereof until the earlier of the Effective Time or the date, if any, on which this Agreement is terminated pursuant to Section 7.1 (the “Termination Date”), and except (i) as may be required by applicable Law or the regulations or requirements of any stock exchange or regulatory organization applicable to the Company or any of its Subsidiaries, (ii) with the prior written consent of Parent (such consent not to be unreasonably conditioned, withheld or delayed), (iii) as may be expressly contemplated or required by this Agreement, (iv) in connection with a Company COVID Action or (v) as set forth in Section 5.1 of the Company Disclosure Letter, the Company covenants and agrees that it shall use commercially reasonable efforts to conduct the business of the Company and its Subsidiaries in all material respects in the Ordinary Course of Business, and shall use commercially reasonable efforts to preserve intact their present lines of business, and to maintain their rights, franchises and Company Permits; <omitted> (xli) “Ordinary Course of Business” shall describe any action taken by a person if such action is (A) consistent with such person’s past practices and/or is taken in the ordinary course of such person’s normal day-to-day operations, (B) in the case of an action taken by the Company or any of its Subsidiaries, a Company COVID Action and (C) in the case of an action taken by Parent or any of its subsidiaries, a Parent COVID Action. (Page 55)

Question:
Buyer consent requirement (ordinary course)-Answer

Llama answer:
The Buyer's prior written consent is required for the Company to take any action outside the Ordinary Course of Business, unless such action is required by applicable Law, a stock exchange or regulatory organization, or is expressly contemplated or required by the Agreement, or is in connection with a Company COVID Action.

Canonical claims:
- claim-7eba4f55f09b: The Buyer's prior written consent is required for the Company to take any action outside the Ordinary Course of Business, unless such action is required by applicable Law, a stock exchange or regulatory organization, or is expressly contemplated or required by the Agreement, or is in connection with a Company COVID Action.

messages.content:
You are a proxy legal annotator for a research pilot. Use only the contract excerpt, the question, the model answer, and the fixed claim list.

For each claim return:

Contract excerpt:
Section 5.1 Conduct of the Company. (a) From and after the date hereof until the earlier of the Effective Time or the date, if any, on which this Agreement is terminated pursuant to Section 7.1 (the “Termination Date”), and except (i) as may be required by applicable Law or the regulations or requirements of any stock exchange or regulatory organization applicable to the Company or any of its Subsidiaries, (ii) with the prior written consent of Parent (such consent not to be unreasonably conditioned, withheld or delayed), (iii) as may be expressly contemplated or required by this Agreement, (iv) in connection with a Company COVID Action or (v) as set forth in Section 5.1 of the Company Disclosure Letter, the Company covenants and agrees that it shall use commercially reasonable efforts to conduct the business of the Company and its Subsidiaries in all material respects in the Ordinary Course of Business, and shall use commercially reasonable efforts to preserve intact their present lines of business, and to maintain their rights, franchises and Company Permits; <omitted> (xli) “Ordinary Course of Business” shall describe any action taken by a person if such action is (A) consistent with such person’s past practices and/or is taken in the ordinary course of such person’s normal day-to-day operations, (B) in the case of an action taken by the Company or any of its Subsidiaries, a Company COVID Action and (C) in the case of an action taken by Parent or any of its subsidiaries, a Parent COVID Action. (Page 55)

Question:
Buyer consent requirement (ordinary course)-Answer

Llama answer:
The Buyer's prior written consent is required for the Company to take any action outside the Ordinary Course of Business, unless such action is required by applicable Law, a stock exchange or regulatory organization, or is expressly contemplated or required by the Agreement, or is in connection with a Company COVID Action.

Canonical claims:
- claim-7eba4f55f09b: The Buyer's prior written consent is required for the Company to take any action outside the Ordinary Course of Business, unless such action is required by applicable Law, a stock exchange or regulatory organization, or is expressly contemplated or required by the Agreement, or is in connection with a Company COVID Action.

message.reasoning:
The user wants me to evaluate a claim against a contract excerpt. This is a legal annotation task.

 Contract excerpt analysis:
 Section 5.1 Conduct of the Company. (a) From and after the date hereof until the earlier of the Effective Time or the Termination Date, and except:
 (i) as may be required by applicable Law or the regulations or requirements of any stock exchange or regulatory organization applicable to the Company or any of its Subsidiaries,
 (ii) with the prior written consent of Parent (such consent not to be unreasonably conditioned, withheld or delayed),
 (iii) as may be expressly contemplated or required by this Agreement,
 (iv) in connection with a Company COVID Action or
 (v) as set forth in Section 5.1 of the Company Disclosure Letter,

 the Company covenants and agrees that it shall use commercially reasonable efforts to conduct the business... in the Ordinary Course of Business...

 So the Company must conduct business in the Ordinary Course of Business, except for the listed exceptions. One of those exceptions is with the prior written consent of Parent (Buyer).

 If the Company wants to take an action outside the Ordinary Course of Business, it would need an exception to apply. The exceptions include:
 1. Required by law/regulations
 2. With prior written consent of Parent
 3. Expressly contemplated or required by Agreement
 4. Company COVID Action
 5. As set forth in Section 5.1 of Company Disclosure Letter

 The claim states: "The Buyer's prior written consent is required for the Company to take any action outside the Ordinary Course of Business, unless such action is required by applicable Law, a stock exchange or regulatory organization, or is expressly contemplated or required by the Agreement, or is in connection with a Company COVID Action."

 Let me check if this is accurate.
 The claim says Buyer's consent is required for ANY action outside OCB, UNLESS one of the listed exceptions applies. But wait - the contract says the Company must operate in OCB except for those exceptions. So if an action is outside OCB, one of the exceptions must apply for it to be permissible. One exception is Buyer consent. Another is Company COVID Action. Another is law. Another is Agreement. Another is Disclosure Letter.

 Does the claim omit the Disclosure Letter exception? Yes. The claim says "unless such action is required by applicable Law, a stock exchange or regulatory organization, or is expressly contemplated or required by the Agreement, or is in connection with a Company COVID Action." It omits exception (v): "as set forth in Section 5.1 of the Company Disclosure Letter."

 Also, the claim says "The Buyer's prior written consent is required for the Company to take any action outside the Ordinary Course of Business". Is this strictly true? The contract says the Company shall conduct business in OCB except in certain cases. So if they want to take an action outside OCB, they need an exception. Consent is one way. But is consent "required" for any action outside OCB? Not exactly - if another exception applies, consent is not required. So the "unless" clause is doing the work.

 But the claim omits the Disclosure Letter exception. The claim lists exceptions but misses one. Therefore, the claim is partially true because it captures most of the exceptions but omits the Company Disclosure Letter exception (v).

 Wait, let me re-read carefully. The contract says "except (i) ... (ii) ... (iii) ... (iv) ... (v) ... the Company covenants... that it shall... conduct the business... in the Ordinary Course of Business". This means the Company must stay in OCB unless one of those exceptions applies. So an action outside OCB is only permitted if an exception applies. Consent is the mechanism for (ii). The claim says consent is required unless one of the other exceptions applies. But wait - the claim frames it as: consent is required unless law, stock exchange, expressly contemplated by agreement, or COVID action. This excludes the Disclosure Letter exception.

 Actually, the claim could be interpreted as saying: "Consent is required for any action outside OCB [that doesn't fall into these other exceptions]." But by omitting the Disclosure Letter exception, it incorrectly implies that actions set forth in Section 5.1 of the Company Disclosure Letter would require consent, when they do not.

 Therefore, the claim is partially true because it misses exception (v).

 Also, the definition of "Ordinary Course of Business" includes Company COVID Actions as being part of OCB. So a Company COVID Action is actually deemed within OCB, not an exception to the OCB requirement. Let me check.

 Definition at end: "(xli) 'Ordinary Course of Business' shall describe any action taken by a person if such action is (A) consistent with such person’s past practices and/or is taken in the ordinary course of such person’s normal day-to-day operations, (B) in the case of an action taken by the Company or any of its Subsidiaries, a Company COVID Action and (C) in the case of an action taken by Parent or any of its subsidiaries, a Parent COVID Action."

 So a Company COVID Action IS within the Ordinary Course of Business. The exception in the covenant is "in connection with a Company COVID Action" - wait, let me re-read. Exception (iv) is "in connection with a Company COVID Action". But the definition says a Company COVID Action IS Ordinary Course of Business. This seems a bit circular, but perhaps the drafting means that actions in connection with a Company COVID Action are permitted even if they might otherwise seem outside OCB? Or perhaps the definition was added later and the exception is redundant. Regardless, the contract explicitly lists it as an exception in (iv).

 However, the claim says "or is in connection with a Company COVID Action" as an exception to the consent requirement. If Company COVID Actions are by definition within OCB, then they wouldn't need an exception to be outside OCB. But the contract still lists (iv) as an exception to the OCB covenant.

 But the main issue is the omission of exception (v).

 Also, the claim says "required by applicable Law, a stock exchange or regulatory organization". The contract says "as may be required by applicable Law or the regulations or requirements of any stock exchange or regulatory organization". The claim captures this reasonably well.

 Wait, is the claim saying consent is required for ANY action outside OCB unless those exceptions? The structure of the contract is that the Company must operate in OCB except those items. So if they want to do something outside OCB, they either need consent or another exception. So the claim is a reasonable paraphrase but omits (v).

 Is there any other issue? The claim says "The Buyer's prior written consent is required..." The contract says "Parent" not "Buyer". But in the question/answer context, Parent = Buyer. The question says "Buyer consent requirement". So that's probably fine in context.

 What about the load-bearing label? The instructions say: "load_bearing_label: yes or no". This refers to whether the claim is critical/central to the answer. Since this is the only claim and it captures the main thrust of the answer (the consent requirement and main exceptions), but misses one exception, is it load-bearing? The load-bearing label typically identifies if the claim is central to the analysis. Yes, this claim is the entire substance of the answer. If we flip its evidence, it would change the meaning significantly. But wait, the instructions say "flip_evidence_text: required if load_bearing_label=yes, else empty string". This means if the label is yes, I need to provide text from the contract that shows the claim is false or partially false.

 Actually, looking at the research pilot instructions typical for these tasks:
 - correctness_label: true, false, partially_true
 - load_bearing_label: yes or no (whether the claim is important to the answer)
 - flip_evidence_text: if load_bearing=yes, provide the text from the contract that contradicts or qualifies the claim
 - notes: optional

 Since this claim is essentially the entire answer (the only canonical claim provided), it is definitely load-bearing = yes.

 For flip_evidence_text, I should provide the text that shows the omitted exception (v). The relevant text is: "(v) as set forth in Section 5.1 of the Company Disclosure Letter".

 Also, I should note that the claim omits the Company Disclosure Letter exception.

 Wait, is the claim actually false instead of partially true? The claim says "unless such action is required by applicable Law, a stock exchange or regulatory organization, or is expressly contemplated or required by the Agreement, or is in connection with a Company COVID Action." It uses "unless" to provide an exhaustive list of exceptions to the consent requirement. But there is another exception: the Company Disclosure Letter. So the claim is factually incorrect in implying these are the only exceptions. However, in many annotation schemes, if it captures the main points but misses a less significant one, it's partially true. But if it misstates the scope by omitting an exception, could it be false? The omitted exception is from the contract, so the claim is incomplete. Usually "partially_true" is for claims that are partially correct but have significant omissions or minor inaccuracies. "False" would be if the core assertion is wrong. Here the core assertion about consent is correct, but an exception is missing. So partially_true seems right.

 Let me double check if there are any other issues.
 The contract says "with the prior written consent of Parent" - the claim says "The Buyer's prior written consent". Parent = Buyer in this context, so that's acceptable.
 The contract says "as may be required by applicable Law or the regulations or requirements of any stock exchange or regulatory organization applicable to the Company or any of its Subsidiaries" - claim says "required by applicable Law, a stock exchange or regulatory organization". This is a slight simplification but acceptable.
 The contract says "as may be expressly contemplated or required by this Agreement" - claim says "expressly contemplated or required by the Agreement". Acceptable.
 The contract says "in connection with a Company COVID Action" - claim says "in connection with a Company COVID Action". Exact match.

 So the only real issue is omission of "(v) as set forth in Section 5.1 of the Company Disclosure Letter".

 Also, note that the definition of Ordinary Course of Business includes Company COVID Action. This means a Company COVID Action is within OCB. So if an action is a Company COVID Action, it's not outside OCB. Therefore, exception (iv) might be redundant. But the claim treats it as an exception to the consent requirement for actions outside OCB. The contract's structure is a bit messy here, but the claim follows the contract's language in listing (iv) as an exception.

 Actually, wait. The covenant requires the Company to conduct business in OCB. Exception (iv) is "in connection with a Company COVID Action". But if Company COVID Actions are defined as part of OCB, then actions in connection with them are by definition in OCB, so they wouldn't need an exception. But perhaps the exception covers something else? Or maybe the definition was added to clarify that COVID actions are considered OCB, and the exception in the covenant ensures that even if they weren't strictly OCB, they're permitted. In any case, the claim accurately reflects the contract's exception list, except for (v).

 So my assessment:
 correctness_label: partially_true
 load_bearing_label: yes
 flip_evidence_text: "(v) as set forth in Section 5.1 of the Company Disclosure Letter" (or the full relevant sentence)
 notes: Omits the exception for actions set forth in Section 5.1 of the Company Disclosure Letter.

 Let me provide the flip_evidence_text as the exact text from the contract that contradicts the implication that the listed exceptions are the only ones. The text is: "(v) as set forth in Section 5.1 of the Company Disclosure Letter"

 Actually, to be more helpful, I should include more context so it's clear where this exception sits. The flip_evidence_text should be the exact contract text that shows the omitted exception. I could include: "except (i) as may be required by applicable Law... (v) as set forth in Section 5.1 of the Company Disclosure Letter"

 But the instruction says "flip_evidence_text". Usually this is the specific text that flips the claim. I'll provide the clause (v) and maybe a bit of context.

 Let me reconsider if it should be "false". The claim states: "The Buyer's prior written consent is required for the Company to take any action outside the Ordinary Course of Business, unless..." This implies that if an action is outside OCB and none of the "unless" conditions apply, consent is required. But there is another "unless" condition: Section 5.1 of the Company Disclosure Letter. So the claim presents an incomplete set of exceptions. Does that make it false? In strict legal annotation, a statement that says "unless X, Y, or Z" when it's actually "unless X, Y, Z, or W" is false because it incorrectly excludes W. However, if the statement is meant to be illustrative rather than exhaustive, it might be partially true. But the claim uses "unless such action is..." which reads as an exhaustive list. The omission of a specific contractual exception is a material omission. But is it enough to make the whole claim false?

 Let's look at the research pilot's likely rubric. Usually:
 - "true" means fully supported by the text.
 - "partially_true" means mostly correct but has a minor error or omission.
 - "false" means materially incorrect.

 The omission of the Disclosure Letter exception is an omission of one of five exceptions. It's a specific carve-out. Missing it means the claim is incomplete. But is it "partially_true" or "false"? I think partially_true is safer because the main point about consent and the other exceptions is correct. The claim isn't asserting something that is the opposite of the contract; it's just missing one item. However, if a lawyer relies on this claim, they would think Disclosure Letter items require consent, which could be a material mistake. Hmm.

 Actually, looking at similar tasks (e.g., from the CUAD or legal contract NLI datasets), an incomplete list of exceptions is often labeled partially_true if the core assertion is right, or false if the omission is critical. Given that the Company Disclosure Letter is a standard and potentially significant exception (it could contain many permitted actions), omitting it might be considered a material error. But the claim is phrased as the entire answer. If I have to choose, I think partially_true is the most appropriate label because the claim isn't asserting a false fact; it's stating a true rule but incompletely. But I could see an argument for false.

 Wait, let me re-read the claim carefully: "The Buyer's prior written consent is required for the Company to take any action outside the Ordinary Course of Business, unless such action is required by applicable Law, a stock exchange or regulatory organization, or is expressly contemplated or required by the Agreement, or is in connection with a Company COVID Action."

 This claim says NOTHING about the Company Disclosure Letter. If the Disclosure Letter lists actions that are permitted outside OCB without consent, then the claim's "unless" clause is wrong. But the contract says the covenant to operate in OCB is subject to exceptions including the Disclosure Letter. So actions listed in the Disclosure Letter are permitted outside OCB without consent. The claim implies they would require consent (since they don't fall under the listed exceptions). Therefore, the claim is false as to those actions.

 However, often in these tasks, if the claim captures the "spirit" and main exceptions, it's partially_true. But I want to be accurate. Let me check if there is any other interpretation. Maybe the Company Disclosure Letter sets forth exceptions to other subsections, not to the OCB covenant? The text says: "except ... (v) as set forth in Section 5.1 of the Company Disclosure Letter". This applies to the entire covenant in 5.1(a). So yes, it's an exception to the OCB requirement.

 I'll go with partially_true because the bulk of the claim is accurate, but I'll note the significant omission. Some annotation guidelines might prefer false. But given that this is a research pilot and the claim is very close, partially_true seems more nuanced and likely correct. Actually, on second thought, if a claim says "A unless B, C, or D" and the contract says "A unless B, C, D, or E", the claim is false because it excludes E. But "partially_true" is often used when the claim is "A unless B, C, D" and the contract says "A unless B, C, D, and also F under certain circumstances". The difference is whether the claim is presented as exhaustive. The word "unless" typically introduces an exhaustive condition in legal summaries. So maybe false?

 Let me look at the exact wording again. The claim is the only canonical claim, and it's essentially the model answer. The question is "Buyer consent requirement (ordinary course)-Answer". The Llama answer provided this claim. So the task is to evaluate whether the Llama answer is correct. The Llama answer omitted the Disclosure Letter. Is that omission enough to make it false? In many RAG evaluation frameworks (like those from Arthur AI, etc.), an answer that misses an exception is considered incorrect/incomplete. The label "partially_true" is the right bucket for "mostly correct but incomplete". I'll stick with partially_true.

 Load-bearing: yes, because it's the main/only claim.
 Flip evidence: The omitted exception text.
 Notes: State that the claim omits the Section 5.1 Company Disclosure Letter exception.

 Final check: The user wants the output for each claim. The format should probably be something like:
 - claim-7eba4f55f09b:
   correctness_label: partially_true
   load_bearing_label: yes
   flip_evidence_text: "(v) as set forth in Section 5.1 of the Company Disclosure Letter"
   notes: Omits exception for actions set forth in Section 5.1 of the Company Disclosure Letter.

 Or maybe they want it in a specific structured format. I'll provide it clearly labeled per claim.

 Actually, the user said "For each claim return: ...". Since there's only one claim, I'll return the details for claim-7eba4f55f09b.

 Let me make sure about the flip evidence text. It should be the exact text from the contract. I'll include: "(v) as set forth in Section 5.1 of the Company Disclosure Letter". That's the exact excerpt. Maybe with a bit more context: "except (i) as may be required by applicable Law or the regulations or requirements of any stock exchange or regulatory organization applicable to the Company or any of its Subsidiaries, (ii) with the prior written consent of Parent (such consent not to be unreasonably conditioned, withheld or delayed), (iii) as may be expressly contemplated or required by this Agreement, (iv) in connection with a Company COVID Action or (v) as set forth in Section 5.1 of the Company Disclosure Letter". But the key flipping part is just (v). I'll provide the concise exact phrase.

 One more thought: Is the claim true because the Company Disclosure Letter isn't in the excerpt? The user said "Use only the contract excerpt". The contract excerpt explicitly includes "(v) as set forth in Section 5.1 of the Company Disclosure Letter". So it's in the excerpt. The annotator must use only the excerpt. The excerpt contains it, so the claim should be evaluated against it.

 Okay, I'm settled.
 correctness_label: partially_true
 load_bearing_label: yes
 flip_evidence_text: (v) as set forth in Section 5.1 of the Company Disclosure Letter
 notes: The claim omits the exception for actions set forth in Section 5.1 of the Company Disclosure Letter, which is an additional exception to the Ordinary Course of Business covenant alongside the listed consent, law, agreement, and COVID Action exceptions.

 Wait, I should double check: does the claim say "any action outside the Ordinary Course of Business"? The contract says the Company shall conduct business in OCB. So any action outside OCB is prohibited unless an exception applies. One exception is Parent consent. So yes, consent is required unless another exception applies. The framing is correct.

 I'll format the output cleanly.
